evidence_quotes.quote: Namely it is the ability of payment processors to censor sites sites by threatening to disable or remove payments on sites unless they remove content which they disagree with personally or image wise.
My ability to use my money, be it via master card or visa, is important to me personally and should not be at the whims of credit card companies.

Secondly, I also want to express my support against video game publishers and studios having the abilities to shut down video games on my machine that I have payed for without any compensation at their discretion.
a game I payed for that I played locally on my own PC was shut down by Ubisoft at their own discretion in 2024 without providing returns or compensation.
If spending 50+ on a game does not mean that I own this game, then what am I spending my money on exactly?

The point is not to force studios to support their games forever. The point is to force studios to either pay consumers for removing access to a game, provide a way for the community to keep a game alive by providing source code for the servers or at least not sue such an effort or to architect the game such that the single player stays playable without access to the servers.

paragraphs.text: I think the digital fairness act is an incredible privilege for consumers and I appreciate being part of the EU greatly for reasons just like this. I think a lot of comments have been left for the preservation of video games, but I wish to add a topic that are equally important to me. Namely it is the ability of payment processors to censor sites sites by threatening to disable or remove payments on sites unless they remove content which they disagree with personally or image wise. While I don't much care about the content removed in this recent case, I think the option to blackmail or hold companies hostage under threat of removing my right to use my money on that platform is anti consumer at it's core. My ability to use my money, be it via master card or visa, is important to me personally and should not be at the whims of credit card companies. Instead, illegal content should be dealt with at a legal level through careful regulation and my right to make purchases with the money I work for protected, not at the mercy of American tech giants and financial institutions with a personal agenda. Secondly, I also want to express my support against video game publishers and studios having the abilities to shut down video games on my machine that I have payed for without any compensation at their discretion. I have fond memories playing the crew with my father, taking turns racing for the best time on a track. Yet a game I payed for that I played locally on my own PC was shut down by Ubisoft at their own discretion in 2024 without providing returns or compensation. If spending 50+ on a game does not mean that I own this game, then what am I spending my money on exactly? I also want to give some perspective on the lobbying against the community in this aspect which I find very disappointing. The point is not to force studios to support their games forever. The point is to force studios to either pay consumers for removing access to a game, provide a way for the community to keep a game alive by providing source code for the servers or at least not sue such an effort or to architect the game such that the single player stays playable without access to the servers. This is all that has been asked and the comments of video game lobbyists aiming to undermine these demands are misrepresenting the demands made by consumers.
